title: Subduction erosion and basal accretion in the Central Chile subduction wedge inferred from local earthquake tomography
journal: Journal of South American Earth Sciences
abstract: Results from a temporary network of 39 seismic stations deployed April–November 2017 in Central Chile reveal 3D elastic wavespeed anomalies using local earthquake tomography. Wedge geometry with body wavespeed anomalies near the interplate contact below 30 km depth are interpreted as accretionary complexes formed by basal accretion of crustal slices. Latitudinal differences are attributed to eroded material from both plates that changes the basalt-eclogite transition depth.

location.description: Central Chile subduction zone 30°–35°S; local earthquake tomography from land+marine network

data.name: 39-Station Temporary Network — Central Chile 30°–35°S (2017) Passive Seismic
data.description: 39-station temporary seismic network (April–November 2017) including possible OBS offshore central Chile; local earthquake tomography reveals 3D velocity model showing basal accretion complexes below 30 km depth

_data_note: Primarily land-based seismic network study. May include offshore components. JSAMES paper; behind paywall.

processing.summary: P- and S-wave arrival times from a 39-station temporary network in central Chile (April–November 2017) were inverted for 3D Vp, Vs and Vp/Vs models via local earthquake tomography. Velocity anomalies at 30+ km depth near the interplate contact are interpreted as basally accreted crustal slices from the subducting Nazca plate. Results show latitudinal variations in accretion/erosion linked to changes in basalt-eclogite transition depth.
workflow: 1. Seismic network installation and data acquisition
processing.notes: Land-based network with possible OBS component offshore; data archive not confirmed

analysis_notes: JSAMES paper; behind paywall. Land/marine network from 2017. Data archive not confirmed. Companion study to CEVICHE-era research.
